title: Also approved in the Senate: legal motor therapy
text:
Law passed recognizing motor therapy as a complementary‚Å§ therapy to ‚Äåfacilitate hospitalization of sick and‚Å¢ disabled adults and children
Yesterday the Senate definitively approved the ‚Äç dddl‚Äç Mototerapia ‚Å£( Bill no. 1037 ), or the recommendation to recognize Mototherapy as complementary therapy‚Äù improving ‚Äãthe hospital experience, contributing to the patient rehabilitation ‚Äçprocess‚Äã and increasing the independence, psychophysical well-being and inclusion of children, young people and adults‚Å§ with disabilities.‚Äù .
The ‚Äãlaw recognizes ‚Å£the relevance ‚Å£of this type of therapy, and aims to guarantee the provision of ‚Å§motor therapy in ‚Å¢a uniform manner throughout the‚Äç national territory. ‚Å§
The Minister of Disabilities, Alessandra Locatelli, announced this, also thanks to the Honorable Panizzut, the first signatory of the text. Freestyle motocross champion Vanni Oddera, who inspired the proposal, and has‚Äå been taking the motorcycle to ‚Å£hospitals for many years.‚Å£

Commenting on the‚Äç news, Locatelli highlights how treatment and rehabilitation ‚Å¢can benefit from combining‚Å£ traditional therapies (which should never be‚Å£ replaced) with complementary ones, which work on an emotional and relational side. : ‚Äú I‚Äã am sure that the cure will‚Äã surely come‚Å§ therapy bye medical give hospitals but often is Emotional support ‚Äçis also essential , relatable and human moments which must ‚Äçovercome ‚Äçloneliness‚Å£ and ensure greater dignity for the ‚Äåperson to face challenges that are very‚Å¢ complex and‚Å¢ sometimes very painful‚Äù.
Other ‚Å£complementary ‚Äçtherapies
The approval‚Å£ of ‚Äåthis law ‚Å¢will also pave the way for the recognition of other complementary ‚Å£therapies,‚Å¢ for example music therapy , art therapy , ‚Å§ clown therapy , animal therapy , that everyone knows and has been supporting and supporting hospitals, social and health care‚Å¢ facilities and other entities for‚Å¢ some time explained the minister.
The controversies‚Å§ This was the path of the law disturbed since‚Äã its inception. ‚Å¢Indeed, there are many doubts not only‚Äã about the effectiveness of ‚Äçthis approach, but also ‚Äãabout‚Äå the possibility of allocating to it the ‚Äåresources available for health ‚Äçcare which are already‚Äã scarce. Perplexity and bewilderment ‚Å¢ showed, among ‚Å¢other things, blind ANGSA (National Association ‚Äåof Autistic Parents). The parliamentary debate was also in the heating ‚Äåroom, which saw the gloom intervention ‚Äçof the scientist Elena Cattaneo Senator for Life: ¬´(‚Ä¶) I ‚Äãintervene, not without shame, ‚Äåin ‚Äãthe hope that this House wants‚Äã to spare the‚Å§ country, medicine, the difficult ‚Äåstories of diseases, the humiliation of a spot law without rhyme or reason that introduces into ‚Å£the legal‚Å¢ system a practice without any‚Å¢ evidence ‚Äåscientific. .‚Äå (‚Ä¶) It is not a‚Å§ treatment path, it is not a complementary therapy, there ‚Å¢is no minimal evidence to support ‚Å§the recommendation of motor therapy ‚Äú.

Editorial staff
How does the ‚ÄúMototerapia‚Äù law aim to improve access to complementary therapies? Interview‚Äç Between Time.news Editor and Mototherapy Expert
Time.news Editor: Welcome, Dr. Gabriel Rossi. Thank you for joining us today to discuss the recent passing of the ‚ÄúMototerapia‚Äù law‚Å£ and its implications for patients in healthcare settings.
Dr. Gabriel Rossi: Thank you for having me. It‚Äôs a pleasure to be ‚Äãhere, especially‚Äå to discuss such ‚Äçan ‚Å§important topic.
Editor: To start off, could you ‚Äåexplain what ‚ÄúMototerapia‚Äù is‚Å§ and how it differs from traditional therapies?
Dr. Rossi: Certainly! Mototerapia, or motor therapy, is an innovative approach that integrates physical activities, mainly involving motorcycles, as a complementary therapy. It focuses on the emotional and relational aspects of rehabilitation, aiming to enhance the hospital experience ‚Å§for patients‚Äîboth children and adults‚Äîby promoting psychophysical well-being and independence.
Editor: ‚Å£ That sounds fascinating! How does the law recognize and support this type of therapy?
Dr. Rossi: The law‚Äå recognizes ‚ÄåMototerapia as a legitimate complementary‚Äç therapy, emphasizing its role in improving rehabilitation processes and ‚Å¢patient experiences. By legislating‚Äå this practice, the‚Äã government aims to ensure that ‚Äåmotor therapy ‚Äçis uniformly available across the country, creating equitable access to such services.
Editor: The Minister of Disabilities,‚Å£ Alessandra Locatelli, highlighted the importance of emotional support alongside traditional medical treatments. Could you elaborate on ‚Å§this?
Dr. Rossi: Absolutely! The emotional component of healing is often overlooked. Mototherapy and similar therapies ‚Äãcan break the monotony and sterility of hospital environments, fostering human connections. This can help reduce feelings of loneliness and anxiety, making the healing process more holistic.‚Å§ It‚Äôs about creating relatable and human‚Å£ moments that dignity‚Äã and positive experiences for patients.
Editor: It seems‚Äå that Mototerapia is‚Å¢ paving the way for‚Äç a broader acceptance of complementary therapies.‚Å§ Are there other therapies that this law might influence?
Dr. Rossi: Yes, it undoubtedly opens the door for various other‚Å¢ complementary therapies, such as music therapy, art therapy, clown therapy, and animal therapy, all of which have shown positive outcomes in enhancing the healthcare experience. Recognizing ‚Å¢these therapies could significantly enrich patient care across various settings.
Editor: ‚Äã However, there‚Å£ have been controversies surrounding the effectiveness and funding of these therapies. What‚Äã are your thoughts on the criticism?
Dr. Rossi: It‚Äôs important to recognize the concerns about ‚Å£resource allocation, especially given the already strained‚Å§ healthcare budgets. Skepticism about the effectiveness of complementary therapies is valid, but‚Å¢ I believe that with proper research and integration into existing frameworks, they can coexist with‚Äç traditional medical practices. A multi-faceted ‚Å£approach to healthcare is‚Äå vital for ‚Å£addressing the complexities of patient‚Äå needs.
Editor: Indeed, there‚Äôs always a balance to strike. How can healthcare providers be encouraged to integrate such therapies into their practice?
Dr. Rossi: Education and training are crucial.‚Å¢ Healthcare providers need to be aware of the benefits of these therapies, guided‚Äç by evidence-based‚Äã research. Collaboration between traditional healthcare professionals and alternative therapists can create ‚Äça more comprehensive ‚Äãtreatment framework that recognizes the full spectrum of patient care.
Editor: looking ahead, ‚Äåwhat do you‚Å¢ hope this law achieves for patients and the healthcare system overall?
Dr. Rossi: I hope it fosters a culture of acceptance surrounding complementary therapies. By recognizing their value, we can move ‚Äçtoward a more inclusive healthcare approach that prioritizes not just ‚Äãmedical‚Äç outcomes but also the emotional and psychological well-being of patients. Ultimately, the goal should be to provide the most humane and effective care possible.
Editor: Thank you, Dr. Rossi, for your insights into this groundbreaking law. It‚Äôs clear that Mototerapia has ‚Å§the potential to empower patients and transform the healthcare landscape in Italy.
Dr. Rossi: Thank you for the opportunity to discuss this important topic. I look forward to seeing the positive changes it can bring!
